title: Tuberculosis: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Identify key diagnostic features of Tuberculosis
Apply clinical reasoning to Tuberculosis management

sections.title: Overview of Tuberculosis
sections.content:
**Tuberculosis**

Latent vs active TB, TST vs IGRA, treatment regimens, MDR-TB approach

**Key Points:**
• Latent TB: positive TST/IGRA, no symptoms, non-infectious, normal CXR
• Active TB: symptoms (cough >3 weeks, hemoptysis, night sweats, weight loss) + abnormal CXR (upper lobe cavitation)
• Diagnosis: sputum AFB smear and culture, nucleic acid amplification test (GeneXpert)

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• Latent TB: positive TST/IGRA, no symptoms, non-infectious, normal CXR
• Active TB: symptoms (cough >3 weeks, hemoptysis, night sweats, weight loss) + abnormal CXR (upper lobe cavitation)

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• Diagnosis: sputum AFB smear and culture, nucleic acid amplification test (GeneXpert)
• Active TB treatment: RIPE (Rifampin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol) × 2 months → RI × 4 months
• Latent TB: isoniazid × 9 months OR rifampin × 4 months; Supplement B6 (pyridoxine) with INH

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for Tuberculosis:**

• Diagnosis: sputum AFB smear and culture, nucleic acid amplification test (GeneXpert)
• Active TB treatment: RIPE (Rifampin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol) × 2 months → RI × 4 months
• Latent TB: isoniazid × 9 months OR rifampin × 4 months; Supplement B6 (pyridoxine) with INH

options.explanation: Latent TB: positive TST/IGRA, no symptoms, non-infectious, normal CXR

options.text: Requires invasive testing in all cases

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Latent TB: positive TST/IGRA, no symptoms, non-infectious, normal CXR.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Latent TB: positive TST/IGRA, no symptoms, non-infectious, normal CXR

coachDx.misconception: While typically self-resolving without treatment may seem plausible, it does not match the clinical scenario described.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Latent TB: positive TST/IGRA, no symptoms, non-infectious, normal CXR from Typically self-resolving without treatment.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Latent TB: positive TST/IGRA, no symptoms, non-infectious, normal CXR.

mcqs.stem: A patient with HIV has a CD4 count of 75 cells/μL and presents with subacute headache, fever, and confusion. India ink stain of CSF is positive. What is the recommended initial treatment?

options.text: IV ceftriaxone + vancomycin for bacterial meningitis coverage
options.explanation: The India ink positivity confirms cryptococcal meningitis. Bacterial meningitis coverage is not indicated when the organism is identified. Antifungal therapy is required.

options.text: IV amphotericin B deoxycholate + flucytosine for 2 weeks (induction), then fluconazole
options.explanation: Positive India ink indicates Cryptococcus neoformans meningitis. Per IDSA/WHO guidelines, induction therapy is amphotericin B + flucytosine × 2 weeks, followed by consolidation with fluconazole 400mg/day × 8 weeks, then maintenance fluconazole 200mg/day. Do NOT start ART immediately — wait 4-6 weeks to avoid immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS).
Fluconazole monotherapy for induction has significantly higher mortality than amphotericin-based regimens. It is used for consolidation and maintenance, not induction.

options.text: Start ART immediately along with amphotericin B
options.explanation: Starting ART simultaneously with antifungal induction significantly increases risk of IRIS, which has high mortality in cryptococcal meningitis. ART should be deferred 4-6 weeks per guidelines.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! IV amphotericin B deoxycholate + flucytosine for 2 weeks (induction), then fluconazole.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: IV amphotericin B deoxycholate + flucytosine for 2 weeks (induction), then fluconazole

coachDx.misconception: Starting ART simultaneously with antifungal induction significantly increases risk of IRIS, which has high mortality in cryptococcal meningitis. ART should be deferred 4-6 weeks per guidelines.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish IV amphotericin B deoxycholate + flucytosine for 2 weeks (induction), then fluco from Start ART immediately along with amphotericin B.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves IV amphotericin B deoxycholate + flucytosine for 2 weeks (induction), then fluconazole.

imaging_reasoning.order_reasoning: Screening and diagnosis; CT if CXR equivocal or extrapulmonary suspected
imaging_reasoning.management_impact: Determines isolation needs, guides sputum collection, distinguishes active vs latent
